comments.text: This question is essentially the same as #367 (although I do note that we look at the powerful part of the product here, as opposed to the product of the powerful parts. But that changes at most a constant). In particular, the construction by Aletheia is also the one found in the comments on #367 (even though they only show unboundedness of the ratio, as opposed to the extra log-term we got here). It is certainly possible that Aletheia came up with it independently, but reading the Aletheia paper they do write that this was done early December 2025, roughly two weeks after the comments on #367. (The site has been updated to address this comment.)

Thanks. Both could be possible, since we do not know system internals of Aletheia. I do think that the explanation of adaptation from #367 is plausible. On the other hand, the paper's own description "This solution is certainly well-known to experts" (because it is short and simple) is not substantiated.
I think it is more the use of the solutions to Pell's equation, which is a standard trick in this area (see the commentary to #364, #365, #366, #672, as well as Wouter's solution to #367).
FYI, yes. I added that line directly on the suggestion of Thomas Bloom. The Pell's equation approach is the standard idea here.

I do think it is important to include the fact that #935 and #367 turned out to contain the same problems, with Aletheia's result already having been obtained in the comment section here. But I guess you agree, since quoting from the paper: it is certainly possible that we missed earlier solutions to these problems by human mathematicians. Therefore, our initial classification into categories is, at best, an upper bound on novelty. It is subject to revision after further investigation by the public.

Yes, I think it was just missed (apparently by everyone, including me and AI) the obvious connection between [367] and [935]. Of course, this connection is obvious once you have them both in mind, but they were added some time apart. I also see now that [367] does not contain the key words 'powerful' or 'squarefull', so presumably when adding this problem (perhaps over a year since [367]) I searched for such key words, saw no similar problems, so added this thinking it was unique. Since then the database is better connected, and I'm wiser to such errors, so hopefully this will be the last such (near) duplicate discovered.

Can you advise on whether we should revise our paper to update the classification of Erdos-935 accordingly? If I understand correctly, the solution to both questions involves the same construction, while the questions are not exactly the same (and are asked on different occasions by Erdos), but essentially equivalent. I guess we already call this solution a "graduate student exercise" which is "well-known to experts". (Also, please feel free to e-mail me to advise further adjustments if necessary; I don't plan on following each problem page.) EDIT: we can confirm that the problem page for Erdos-367 was not accessed in the thinking logs for the solution to Erdos-935. Also, the date of the comments on Erdos-367 is after the knowledge cutoff for training.

Based on your edit I think we can safely conclude that Aletheia came up with it independently, which is rather exciting. I am however slightly surprised then that Aletheia did not think of the exact same idea when considering #367, but okay. In any case, I would now argue that it's more appropriately placed in Section 4 of your paper, rather than Section 3. Although I do understand if you would ideally like to keep Section 4 for independent discoveries that fully settle an Erdős problem as written here. That being said, what counts as a 'full' or 'partial' solution can be rather arbitrary. It may hinge on the exact way Thomas decides to write down the problem (of which there is a often more than one sensible approach), and whether several related questions get their own problem page or not.

On #367: the attempt was self-filtered out in the initial 700 -> 212 stage, so humans never saw the output. I dug it up from the logs: Aletheia found the Erdos problem page and acknowledged not solving the problem completely. For #935, Aletheia incorrectly asserted solving the whole problem, which is why the solution to part (2) reached human eyes.
Might there be more interesting partial progress on other problems in the other responses?

Yes, that is probably the case. In fact, someone on our team pointed out that the solution candidate to Erdos-281 was arguably close to correct. Given the pace of improvements to the base model and agent, though, it would be more efficient for a team of our size to keep rerunning the open problems rather than examining old outputs. Personally I would only be inclined to revisit the Erdos problems after improving the model's capability substantially. To find more problems that can be solved with the current capability, I think it would be better to just give our tools to the public. The Erdos problems were actually not a consideration when building Aletheia; we ran them later when it became clear that they would become a common discussion point. It turned out to be useful for us to get a large-scale picture of the model's capabilities, but I think our team is happy to leave the results as a case study instead of trying to optimize the number of Erdos problems solved. We each have our own pet research problems that we want to get back to :) P.S. Tonight's arXiv update will move our Erdos-935 result to the "Independent Rediscovery" section, crediting Woett appropriately.

> Given the pace of improvements to the base model and agent, > though, it would be more efficient for a team of our size to > keep rerunning the open problems rather than examining old > outputs. That reminds me of a Science Fiction novel from the 1970's: Humanity starts spaceships to another star system, with the crew in cryotanks. Twenty years after launch technique on earth has developed, and newly started spaceships (again with crew in cryo-tanks) will overtake the ships of generation 1. And another 20 years later a third generation of space ships will be even faster: they will overtake also the ships of generation 2... > Personally I would only be inclined to revisit the Erdos problems > after improving the model's capability substantially. To find more > problems that can be solved with the current capability, ... > I think it would be better to just give our tools to the public. Do that. I am a member of this public and it is my wish: Do that! We live in exciting times!
